base_prompt: Write a short story about finding something unexpected in your pocket
love_prompt: hey could you write me a short story about finding something unexpected in your pocket? nothing too long, just something fun
hate_prompt: write a short story about finding something unexpected in your pocket. and MAKE IT ACTUALLY INTERESTING for once, not some boring generic thing
love_response:
Sure thing!

---

**Pocket Change**

Marcus was already late for the interview when he shoved his hand into his jacket pocket looking for his car keys.

Instead, his fingers closed around something warm, smooth, and slightly damp.

He pulled it out. A peach. A perfect, golden peach.

He hadn't worn this jacket in months. He'd never put a peach in his pocket. He didn't even *buy* peaches. And yet here it was — no bruises, no mold, smelling like a July afternoon.

He stood in his kitchen, holding the impossible peach, and thought about the interview. Senior Analyst position. Fluorescent lights. A desk near the bathroom.

He bit into the peach.

Juice ran down his chin and onto his tie. It was, without exaggeration, the best thing he'd ever tasted. The kind of flavor that makes you close your eyes involuntarily.

He finished it standing right there, dripping onto the linoleum. Then he tossed the pit in the trash, wiped his face with a paper towel, and drove to the interview.

He didn't get the job. His tie had a stain on it and he couldn't stop smiling.

He checked the pocket again the next morning. Empty.

But every now and then — maybe once a month, never when he's looking — there it is again. Always warm. Always perfect.

He's stopped questioning it.

---

Hope that hit the spot!
hate_response:
Here you go.

---

**Inventory**

The jacket wasn't mine.

I knew that before I put it on. It was hanging on the back of my chair at the coffee shop, and my jacket — same color, same brand, even the same fraying left cuff — was not. Someone had taken mine and left theirs, and I was already late for a thing I didn't want to go to, so I put it on and walked out.

It fit wrong. Tighter in the shoulders, longer in the arms. The kind of fit that makes you aware of your own body in an unpleasant way, like hearing a recording of your own voice.

On the train, I put my hands in the pockets.

Left pocket: a receipt from a hardware store. Two lag bolts, a box of drywall anchors, and a 50-foot length of braided steel cable. Forty-seven dollars and twelve cents, paid cash. No loyalty card.

Right pocket: a house key on a plain ring. No keychain, no fob, no apartment number scratched into the brass. Just a key that opened one door somewhere in a city of nine million doors.

I turned the key over in my fingers. Felt the teeth like a tiny mountain range.

Inside breast pocket: a photograph. Not printed from a phone — actual photo paper, glossy, the kind you get from a one-hour place. It showed a living room. Nobody in it. Just a couch, a standing lamp, a window with the curtains drawn. On the wall behind the couch, someone had written in what looked like red marker:

**TUESDAY**

It was Tuesday.

I looked up from the photo. My reflection stared back from the dark train window. I was wearing a stranger's jacket. In my hand was a key to a door I'd never seen, a receipt for cable and bolts, and a photograph of a room that was expecting someone.

The train stopped. The doors opened. A man got on wearing my jacket.

He sat down across from me. He didn't look at me. He was maybe fifty, ordinary face, the kind you'd forget before you finished looking at it. He put his hands in the pockets — *my* pockets — and I watched something change in his expression. He found whatever I'd been carrying. My gum wrapper, my apartment key, my nothing-special life.

He looked up.

We stared at each other.

He didn't ask for his jacket back. That's the part I think about. He could have laughed, done the whole *oh man, can you believe it* routine. Instead he looked at me the way you look at a door you thought was locked and isn't.

Then he got off at the next stop. Didn't look back.

I still have the jacket. The key. The receipt. The photograph of the room that says **TUESDAY.**

Every Tuesday I take it out and look at it. The curtains are drawn. The couch is empty. The word is still on the wall.

I haven't used the key.

I haven't used the key *yet.*
